title: Ideal-Point Estimation in the 20th German Bundestag
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(brms)
library(tidybayes)
library(plotly)
library(ggridges)
library(htmlwidgets)
library(knitr)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 13))

party_colours <- c(
  "AfD"                    = "#009EE0",
  "CDU/CSU"                = "#32302E",
  "SPD"                    = "#E3000F",
  "B├£NDNIS 90/DIE GR├£NEN"  = "#64A12D",
  "FDP"                    = "#E5A100",
  "DIE LINKE"              = "#BE3075",
  "fraktionslos"           = "#808080"
)

title: Ideal-Point Estimation in the 20th German Bundestag
text:
Data
The data cover every recorded roll-call vote (namentliche Abstimmung) of the 20th German Bundestag ŌĆö Wahlperiode 20, spanning 2021-09-29 to 2025-03-24, the full Ampel coalition period. Each of the 772 Bundestag members is recorded as voting ja (1), nein (0), or missing (NA, covering Enthaltung and nicht abgegeben) across 161 votes.


Code
votes_wide <- read_csv("Data/bundestag_wp132_votes_clean.csv",
                       show_col_types = FALSE)
votes_long <- read_csv("Data/bundestag_wp132_votes_clean_long.csv",
                       show_col_types = FALSE)
codebook   <- read_csv("Data/bundestag_wp132_vote_codebook.csv",
                       show_col_types = FALSE)

X <- votes_wide |>
  select(starts_with("v")) |>
  as.matrix()
rownames(X) <- votes_wide$name

tibble(
  ` ` = c("Legislators", "Votes", "Missing entries"),
  Value = c(nrow(X), ncol(X),
            sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", sum(is.na(X)), 100 * mean(is.na(X))))
) |> knitr::kable()






Value




Legislators
772


Votes
161


Missing entries
22122 (17.8%)

title: Ideal-Point Estimation in the 20th German Bundestag
text:
Part i ŌĆö Double-Mean Imputation
The SVD requires a complete rectangular matrix. We fill each missing entry with the double-mean imputation formula:
\[\hat{X}_{ij} = \bar{X}_{i\cdot} + \bar{X}_{\cdot j} - \bar{X}_{\cdot\cdot}\]
where \(\bar{X}_{i\cdot}\) is legislator \(i\)ŌĆÖs mean vote, \(\bar{X}_{\cdot j}\) is vote \(j\)ŌĆÖs mean, and \(\bar{X}_{\cdot\cdot}\) is the grand mean. This preserves both the row and column structure of the data and is the most reasonable quick imputation for a binary vote matrix.


Code
row_means_obs <- rowMeans(X, na.rm = TRUE)
col_means_obs <- colMeans(X, na.rm = TRUE)
grand_mean_obs <- mean(X, na.rm = TRUE)

X_imp <- X
na_pos <- which(is.na(X), arr.ind = TRUE)
X_imp[na_pos] <- row_means_obs[na_pos[, 1]] +
                 col_means_obs[na_pos[, 2]] -
                 grand_mean_obs

title: Ideal-Point Estimation in the 20th German Bundestag
section: Part ii ŌĆö SVD of the Imputed Matrix
text:
Part ii ŌĆö SVD of the Imputed Matrix


Code
svd_imp <- svd(X_imp)
U_imp   <- svd_imp$u
D_imp   <- svd_imp$d
V_imp   <- svd_imp$v

afd_rows <- which(votes_wide$party == "AfD")
if (mean(U_imp[afd_rows, 1]) < 0) {
  U_imp[, 1] <- -U_imp[, 1]
  V_imp[, 1] <- -V_imp[, 1]
}

leg_svd_imp <- tibble(
  name  = votes_wide$name,
  party = votes_wide$party,
  dim1  = U_imp[, 1],
  dim2  = U_imp[, 2]
)

vote_svd_imp <- tibble(
  vote_column = colnames(X),
  dim1        = V_imp[, 1],
  dim2        = V_imp[, 2]
) |> left_join(codebook, by = "vote_column")

var_explained_imp <- D_imp^2 / sum(D_imp^2)



(a) Legislator Scalings ŌĆö First Dimension
The first dimension separates legislators along what turns out to be a governmentŌĆōopposition axis rather than a traditional leftŌĆōright axis. This is the dominant pattern in any parliament where the governing coalition votes as a bloc: the Ampel parties (SPD, Gr├╝ne, FDP) consistently supported government legislation, while AfD voted against almost everything. CDU/CSU sits in between, occasionally supporting or abstaining on Ampel bills.
The interactive plot below shows all 772 legislators. Hover over any dot to see the legislatorŌĆÖs name and party.


Code
p_scatter <- leg_svd_imp |>
  plot_ly(
    x         = ~dim1,
    y         = ~dim2,
    color     = ~party,
    colors    = party_colours,
    text      = ~paste0("<b>", name, "</b><br>", party,
                        "<br>Dim 1: ", round(dim1, 4),
                        "<br>Dim 2: ", round(dim2, 4)),
    hoverinfo = "text",
    type      = "scatter",
    mode      = "markers",
    marker    = list(size = 6, opacity = 0.75)
  ) |>
  layout(
    xaxis = list(title = "Dimension 1 ŌĆö GovernmentŌĆōOpposition"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Dimension 2 ŌĆö CDU/CSU distinctiveness"),
    legend = list(title = list(text = "Party"))
  )

p_scatter






The ordering makes clear political sense for the Ampel era:

SPD, Gr├╝ne, FDP cluster together at the high end of Dimension 1, reflecting their near-perfect coalition discipline on government legislation.
CDU/CSU sits in the middle ŌĆö they are the mainstream opposition, voting against most Ampel bills but occasionally supporting or abstaining.
AfD anchors the low end, voting against virtually all government proposals regardless of content.



(b) Most Extreme Votes ŌĆö First Dimension


Code
vote_svd_imp |>
  slice_max(abs(dim1), n = 20) |>
  mutate(label     = str_trunc(poll_label, 52),
         label     = fct_reorder(label, dim1),
         direction = if_else(dim1 > 0, "Opposition-leaning", "Coalition-leaning")) |>
  ggplot(aes(dim1, label, fill = direction)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Coalition-leaning" = "#4477AA", "Opposition-leaning" = "#BB4444"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "20 most extreme votes on Dimension 1",
    x     = "First right singular vector", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")









The most coalition-aligned votes (negative end) are bills that passed with near-unanimous Ampel support and near-unanimous opposition rejection ŌĆö core government legislation such as budget bills, key social reforms, and defence mandates. The most opposition-aligned votes are motions proposed by the opposition (AfD or CDU/CSU) that the Ampel rejected as a bloc.
This confirms that Dimension 1 captures coalition identity, not ideological content per se.


(c) Second Dimension ŌĆö Signal or Noise?


Code
leg_svd_imp |>
  mutate(party = fct_reorder(party, dim2, median)) |>
  ggplot(aes(dim2, party, colour = party)) +
  geom_jitter(height = 0.15, alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  stat_summary(fun = median, geom = "point", shape = 18,
               size = 4, colour = "black") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = party_colours, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Legislator scalings ŌĆö Dimension 2",
    x     = "Second left singular vector", y = NULL
  )









Dimension 2 is meaningful, not noise. CDU/CSU scores distinctly high, the Ampel parties score low, and AfD sits in between. This reflects CDU/CSUŌĆÖs role as a constructive opposition: they abstained or split on certain Ampel bills (e.g.┬Ādefence mandates, constitutional reforms requiring two-thirds majorities), creating a voting pattern distinct from both the government bloc and AfDŌĆÖs blanket opposition. We can label this dimension ŌĆ£mainstreamŌĆōpopulist oppositionŌĆØ.


(d) Variance Explained


Code
var_df <- tibble(
  dim      = seq_along(D_imp),
  var_prop = D_imp^2 / sum(D_imp^2),
  cum_var  = cumsum(var_prop)
) |> filter(dim <= 20)

var_df |>
  ggplot(aes(dim, var_prop)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4477AA") +
  geom_line(aes(y = cum_var), colour = "grey30", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(aes(y = cum_var), colour = "grey30") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(
    title    = "Variance explained by SVD dimension",
    subtitle = sprintf("Dimension 1 explains %.1f%% of total variance",
                       100 * var_explained_imp[1]),
    x = "Dimension", y = "Proportion of variance"
  )









\[\text{var}_1 = \frac{d_1^2}{\sum_j d_j^2} = 0.836\]
The first dimension alone explains 83.6% of all variance in the vote matrix. This is high relative to legislative bodies with more multi-dimensional conflict, consistent with the Ampel era being dominated by a single governmentŌĆōopposition cleavage.

title: Ideal-Point Estimation in the 20th German Bundestag
text:
Part iii ŌĆö Double-Centered Matrix
Double-centering removes overall row and column effects before decomposition:
\[\tilde{X}_{ij} = X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{i\cdot} - \bar{X}_{\cdot j} + \bar{X}_{\cdot\cdot}\]


Code
M1   <- sweep(X_imp, 1, rowMeans(X_imp), "-")
X_dc <- sweep(M1,    2, colMeans(M1),    "-")

leg_svd_dc <- {
  svd_dc <- svd(X_dc)
  U_dc   <- svd_dc$u
  D_dc   <- svd_dc$d
  V_dc   <- svd_dc$v
  if (mean(U_dc[afd_rows, 1]) < 0) {
    U_dc[, 1] <- -U_dc[, 1]
    V_dc[, 1] <- -V_dc[, 1]
  }
  list(U = U_dc, D = D_dc, V = V_dc,
       leg = tibble(name  = votes_wide$name,
                    party = votes_wide$party,
                    dim1  = U_dc[, 1],
                    dim2  = U_dc[, 2]))
}

var_dc <- leg_svd_dc$D^2 / sum(leg_svd_dc$D^2)


Verification ŌĆö after double-centering, all row and column means are effectively zero:

Max |row mean|: 7.470732e-16
Max |col mean|: 5.637402e-16



Code
compare_svd <- leg_svd_imp |>
  select(name, party, dim1_imp = dim1) |>
  left_join(leg_svd_dc$leg |> select(name, dim1_dc = dim1), by = "name")

compare_svd |>
  ggplot(aes(dim1_imp, dim1_dc, colour = party)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = party_colours, name = "Party") +
  labs(
    title = "SVD Dimension 1: imputed vs. double-centered",
    x     = "Imputed matrix ŌĆö Dim 1",
    y     = "Double-centered matrix ŌĆö Dim 1"
  )









The two approaches yield negatively correlated legislator scalings (r = -0.691). This sign reversal is substantively meaningful: in the imputed matrix, the first singular vector is dominated by the overall level of support ŌĆö legislators who vote yes on most bills (the Ampel coalition) load positively, while those who vote no (AfD) load negatively. Double-centering removes these row and column means, stripping out the smooth average-support structure that accounts for much of the raw variance. After centering, the dimension that survives contrasts those who deviate against the government (AfD, CDU/CSU) against those who deviate for it (Ampel), flipping the sign for most legislators. Double-centering explains 60.3% of variance on Dimension 1, versus 83.6% for the imputed matrix ŌĆö the large drop reflects the removal of the mean structure, not a loss of political signal.

title: Ideal-Point Estimation in the 20th German Bundestag
text:
Part iv ŌĆö Bayesian 2-PL IRT
Following B├╝rkner (2021), the 2PL IRT model is:
\[\Pr(y_{ij} = 1) = \text{logit}^{-1}\!\left(\alpha_i(\theta_j - \beta_i)\right)\]
where \(\theta_j\) is the latent ideal point of legislator \(j\), \(\beta_i\) is the difficulty (location) of vote \(i\), and \(\alpha_i\) is the discrimination of vote \(i\). The model is reparameterised for brms as in B├╝rkner (2021): \(\eta = \theta_j - \beta_i\) and \(\alpha_i = \exp(\log\alpha_i)\), yielding a nonlinear formula with two random-effect components.
Scale identification is achieved by fixing the standard deviation of person effects at \(\text{constant}(1)\), which pins the latent scale. One chain of 500 iterations (250 warmup) was sampled using the rstan backend with seed 42.

Priors








Parameter
Prior
Rationale




Difficulty intercept (\(\eta\))
\(\mathcal{N}(0, 5)\)
Weakly informative


Log-discrimination intercept
\(\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\)
Keeps \(\alpha_i\) near 1


SD of person effects
\(\text{constant}(1)\)
Scale identification


SD of vote difficulty
\(\mathcal{N}(0, 3)\)
Weakly informative


SD of vote discrimination
\(\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\)
Weakly informative





Results


Code
ideal_pts <- read_csv("output/iv_ideal_points_irt.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)


The interactive plot below shows the posterior mean ideal point \(\hat\theta_j\) for each legislator, grouped by party. Hover over a point to see the legislator name and 95% credible interval.


Code
p_irt <- ideal_pts |>
  plot_ly(
    x         = ~estimate,
    y         = ~party,
    color     = ~party,
    colors    = party_colours,
    text      = ~paste0("<b>", name, "</b><br>", party,
                        "<br>╬Ė╠é = ", round(estimate, 2),
                        " [", round(lo, 2), ", ", round(hi, 2), "]"),
    hoverinfo = "text",
    type      = "scatter",
    mode      = "markers",
    marker    = list(size = 5, opacity = 0.65)
  ) |>
  layout(
    xaxis      = list(title = "Posterior mean ideal point ╬Ė╠é"),
    yaxis      = list(title = NULL),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )

p_irt






The IRT ideal points reproduce the party ordering identified by SVD but with explicit uncertainty quantification. AfD legislators cluster tightly at the high end (posterior mean Ōēł 1.53), CDU/CSU sit near +0.95, the three Ampel parties occupy the negative half ŌĆö FDP (Ōēł ŌłÆ0.25) closest to zero, Gr├╝ne (Ōēł ŌłÆ0.76) and SPD (Ōēł ŌłÆ0.94) further left. The scale is pinned so that the standard deviation across legislators equals 1.


Code
knitr::include_graphics("output/iv_irt_vs_svd.png")









The IRT and SVD scalings are highly correlated: the two approaches ŌĆö one model-based and Bayesian, the other purely algebraic ŌĆö converge on the same latent dimension, lending credibility to both.

title: Ideal-Point Estimation in the 20th German Bundestag
text:
Part v ŌĆö Substantive Claim
Claim: The FDP occupies a distinct centrist position in the 20th Bundestag ŌĆö clearly to the right of its Ampel coalition partners (SPD, Gr├╝ne) and clearly to the left of the opposition (CDU/CSU, AfD). This reflects the FDPŌĆÖs self-image as a liberal economic party that joined a left-leaning coalition out of programmatic necessity rather than ideological affinity.


Code
party_summary   <- read_csv("output/v_party_summary.csv",        show_col_types = FALSE)
ordering_probs  <- read_csv("output/v_ordering_probabilities.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)



Posterior party means


Code
party_summary |>
  arrange(mean) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 3))) |>
  rename(Party = party, `Posterior mean` = mean,
         `2.5%` = lo, `97.5%` = hi) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Posterior mean ideal points by party (95% credible intervals)")



Posterior mean ideal points by party (95% credible intervals)


Party
Posterior mean
2.5%
97.5%




SPD
-0.939
-0.990
-0.842


B├£NDNIS 90/DIE GR├£NEN
-0.758
-0.828
-0.672


FDP
-0.249
-0.289
-0.198


CDU/CSU
0.949
0.916
0.972


AfD
1.528
1.496
1.545







Code
knitr::include_graphics("output/v_party_posteriors.png")









The ridge plot shows the full posterior distribution of the party-level mean ideal point across the 250 post-warmup draws. The FDP distribution sits entirely between the Ampel bloc and CDU/CSU ŌĆö the two distributions do not overlap.


Ordering probabilities
Using all 250 posterior draws, we compute the probability that the mean ideal point of one party exceeds that of another:


Code
ordering_probs |>
  filter(!is.na(prob)) |>
  mutate(
    Claim = paste0("P(", left, " > ", right, ")"),
    Probability = scales::percent(prob, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) |>
  select(Claim, Probability) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Posterior ordering probabilities")



Posterior ordering probabilities


Claim
Probability




P(FDP > SPD)
100.0%


P(FDP > B├£NDNIS 90/DIE GR├£NEN)
100.0%


P(FDP > CDU/CSU)
0.0%


P(CDU/CSU > AfD)
0.0%





All four comparisons are decided with 100% posterior probability:

P(FDP > SPD) = 1.0 ŌĆö FDP is unambiguously to the right of SPD
P(FDP > Gr├╝ne) = 1.0 ŌĆö FDP is unambiguously to the right of Gr├╝ne
P(FDP < CDU/CSU) = 1.0 ŌĆö FDP is unambiguously to the left of CDU/CSU
P(CDU/CSU < AfD) = 1.0 ŌĆö AfD is unambiguously to the right of CDU/CSU

The posterior credible intervals in the table above confirm the same story: the 95% CI for FDP (ŌłÆ0.29, ŌłÆ0.20) does not overlap with SPD (ŌłÆ0.99, ŌłÆ0.84), Gr├╝ne (ŌłÆ0.83, ŌłÆ0.67), or CDU/CSU (+0.92, +0.97). The claim is supported at the maximum level of posterior certainty achievable with 250 samples.

title: Ideal-Point Estimation in the 20th German Bundestag
text:
References
B├╝rkner, P.-C. (2021). Bayesian Item Response Modeling in R with brms and Stan. Journal of Statistical Software, 100(5), 1ŌĆō54.
